aliases: Steamed glutinous rice with coconut custard

english: Steamed glutinous rice with coconut custard

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Pulut Srikaya; closest defensible match is glutinous rice cake food product (FOODON_03301710). The custard topping layer is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based sweet snack / kueh — steamed glutinous rice with coconut milk custard topping
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Pulut Srikaya. A0CKP (rice-based snack) is the closest defensible anchor. Custard topping component is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Pulut Srikaya or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pulut Srikaya is one piece weighing approximately 80 g, consisting of a layer of steamed glutinous rice topped with a set coconut-egg custard.

nutrient_source.title: Pulut Srikaya — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pulut Srikaya (F11090047). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution (~81% of total fat is saturated in coconut milk); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pulut Srikaya as a composite dish. GI of 86 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (sticky) rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor (GI ~86, high category). The added sugar and absence of dietary fibre reinforce the high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (86 × 33.98) / 100 ≈ 29.2 g (80 g serving × 42.48 g carbohydrate/100 g = 33.98 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (42.48 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Pulut Srikaya; despite its moderate sodium content (72 mg/100 g), the 42.48 g/100 g sugar load with no fibre triggers insulin-driven sodium retention that independently elevates blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 42.48 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; glutinous rice base has a GI of ~86 and zero dietary fibre, producing a severe and rapid postprandial blood glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Pulut Srikaya or consume only a very small portion on rare occasions, as the combination of high-GI glutinous rice (GI ~86), 42.48 g/100 g sugar, and zero dietary fibre creates one of the highest glycaemic load profiles among traditional kueh.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of ~9.2 g/100 g from coconut milk, combined with 42.48 g/100 g sugar and zero fibre, raises LDL cholesterol and promotes atherogenic and inflammatory risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Pulut Srikaya; the high saturated fat (~9.2 g/100 g from coconut milk) raises LDL cholesterol, while the extreme sugar content (42.48 g/100 g) promotes triglyceride elevation and systemic inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 42.48 g/100 g far exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold; saturated fat at ~9.2 g/100 g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Pulut Srikaya; the extremely high sugar content (42.48 g/100 g) strongly promotes inflammatory mediators, and coconut milk-derived saturated fat (~9.2 g/100 g) further drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pulut Srikaya is a traditional Peranakan and Malay steamed kueh (rice cake) widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia as a breakfast item or snack. The kueh consists of two distinct layers: a dense, chewy base of glutinous (sticky) rice steamed with coconut milk, which gives it a rich, creamy flavour and a characteristic sticky texture; and a set upper layer of yellow custard (kaya-style topping) made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, wheat flour, and yellow food colouring. The custard layer is smooth, slightly firm, and sweet, contrasting with the denser rice base. Pulut Srikaya is typically sold in squares or rectangles and is a staple at Peranakan kueh stalls, hawker centres, and traditional bakeries across Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 295 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate load of 42.48 g/100 g — all of which is reported as sugar — and a moderate fat content of 11.32 g/100 g, predominantly saturated fat (~9.2 g/100 g) from coconut milk. Protein is 5.69 g/100 g from eggs and coconut milk. Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), and sodium is moderate at 72 mg/100 g. The glutinous rice base has a very high glycemic index (~86), and combined with the added sugar and zero fibre, this food produces a severe postprandial blood glucose spike. It is clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A typical serving is one piece (~80 g), delivering approximately 236 kcal and a glycemic load of ~29, placing it among the highest-risk traditional kueh for metabolic health.

What is the GI of pulut srikaya?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090047), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice (the dominant carbohydrate source), which is the approved GI source. Saturated fat is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk and is documented as an estimation.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — glutinous rice and the custard topping as prepared contain negligible dietary fibre. saturated_fat_g of 9.2 g/100 g is estimated: coconut milk constitutes the primary fat source in both the rice base and custard layer; coconut milk fat is approximately 81% saturated, applied to total fat of 11.32 g/100 g yields ~9.2 g/100 g saturated fat. This is an estimation and not directly reported by HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pulut Srikaya (F11090047) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Pulut Srikaya as a composite dish exists in approved sources. The sugar value (42.48 g/100 g) equals total carbohydrate, which is as reported by HPB; this may reflect the combined contribution of starch-derived sugars and added sugar in the HPB analytical method rather than exclusively free sugars — users should interpret with caution.
